text: Sorting Pattern: Merge sort. Divide array in half recursively, merge sorted halves. Stable sort. Time: O(n log n) all cases, Space: O(n). Use when stability required or sorting linked lists (no random access). Merge step: two-pointer merge of sorted halves. Key advantage: predictable performance, good for external sorting with large datasets.

Sorting Pattern: Quick sort. Pick pivot, partition array so elements < pivot are left, > pivot are right. Recurse on both halves. Time: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case (sorted input with bad pivot). Space: O(log n) stack. Use random pivot or median-of-three to avoid worst case. In-place, cache-friendly, faster in practice than merge sort.

Sorting Pattern: Heap sort. Build max-heap in O(n), repeatedly extract max and place at end. Time: O(n log n) all cases, Space: O(1) in-place. Not stable. Use when O(1) extra space is required. Build heap with heapify (sift-down from n//2 to 0). Extract: swap root with last, reduce heap size, sift-down root.

Sorting Pattern: Counting sort. For integer keys in range [0, k]. Count occurrences, compute prefix sums for positions, place elements. Time: O(n+k), Space: O(n+k). Use when k=O(n). Not comparison-based; beats O(n log n) lower bound. Stable when done correctly. Example: sort array with values 0..100 in O(n) time.

Sorting Pattern: Radix sort. Sort integers digit by digit from LSD to MSD using stable counting sort. Time: O(d*(n+k)) where d=digits, k=radix. For 32-bit integers: 4 passes with radix 256, O(4n). Space: O(n+k). Outperforms comparison sorts for large n with bounded integer keys. Use for: sorting large integer arrays, strings of equal length.

Sorting Pattern: Bucket sort. Distribute elements into buckets by range, sort each bucket, concatenate. Time: O(n+k) average when elements uniformly distributed, O(n²) worst case. Space: O(n+k). Use when input is uniformly distributed over a range (e.g., sort floats in [0,1]). Each bucket typically has O(1) elements on average.

Sorting Pattern: Dutch National Flag (3-way partition). Sort array of three distinct values (0,1,2). Three pointers: lo, mid, hi. If arr[mid]==0: swap with lo, advance both. If arr[mid]==1: advance mid. If arr[mid]==2: swap with hi, decrement hi. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Use for: sort colors, partition around pivot with duplicates in QuickSort.

Sorting Pattern: Topological sort (ordering with dependencies). Sort DAG nodes so all edges go forward. DFS-based (post-order) or BFS-based (Kahn's). Use when items have prerequisite relationships. Not comparison-based. Time: O(V+E). Detects cycles (impossible ordering). Use for: build systems, course scheduling, dependency resolution.

Sorting Pattern: Custom comparator sort. Define comparison function for complex ordering. Python: sort with key=lambda x: (x[1], -x[0]) for multi-key sort. For custom objects: implement __lt__. Avoid unstable comparators (can cause non-deterministic behavior). Use functools.cmp_to_key to convert comparator to key function. Time: O(n log n).

Sorting Pattern: Sort by frequency. Count occurrences, then sort by frequency descending (and by value for ties). Use collections.Counter and sorted with key=(-count, value). Time: O(n log n). Use for: top K frequent elements, sort characters by frequency, reorganize string. Alternative for top K: use heap for O(n log k).

Sorting Pattern: Merge intervals (sort-based). Sort intervals by start time, then sweep to merge overlapping ones. Time: O(n log n). Use: merged intervals are contiguous in sorted order, so single pass suffices after sorting. Also used in: insert interval (binary search for position), non-overlapping intervals (sort by end, greedy).

Sorting Pattern: Kth largest element. QuickSelect: partition around pivot, recurse only on relevant side. Time: O(n) average, O(n²) worst case. Use median-of-medians for O(n) worst case. Alternative: min-heap of size k: push all elements, return heap top. Time: O(n log k). For streaming data, heap approach is better. For offline, QuickSelect is faster.

Sorting Pattern: Inversion count (modified merge sort). Count pairs (i,j) where i<j but arr[i]>arr[j]. During merge step: when right element is placed before remaining left elements, add count of remaining left elements. Time: O(n log n). Also: BIT/Fenwick tree approach processes elements right-to-left, counts smaller elements to the right. Time: O(n log n).

Sorting Pattern: Sort nearly sorted array (k-sorted). Each element is at most k positions away from sorted position. Use min-heap of size k+1. Insert first k+1 elements, then for each new element: pop min (it's in final position), push new. Time: O(n log k). Better than O(n log n) general sort when k << n.

Sorting Pattern: Wiggle sort. Arrange array so arr[0]<=arr[1]>=arr[2]<=arr[3]... Simple O(n): for each pair, if order is wrong, swap. Guaranteed to produce valid wiggle arrangement. For wiggle sort II (strict): median partition + index mapping to avoid same elements being adjacent. Time: O(n) for basic, O(n) average with 3-way partition for strict.

Sorting Pattern: Pancake sorting. Sort array using only prefix reversals. Find max element in unsorted portion, reverse to bring to front, reverse to move to correct position. Repeat. Number of flips: 2*(n-1) maximum. Time: O(n²) flips, O(n) per flip. Used in: problems modeling physical sorting with limited operations.

Sorting Pattern: External sort (merge sort for large data). When data doesn't fit in memory. Phase 1: read chunks, sort in memory, write sorted runs to disk. Phase 2: k-way merge of sorted runs using min-heap. Time: O(n log n), I/O: O(n/B * log(n/M)) where B=block size, M=memory. Use for: database sorting, MapReduce, large log file processing.

Sorting Pattern: Relative sort array. Sort arr1 such that elements appear in order defined by arr2; elements not in arr2 go to end in ascending order. Use order map: {val: idx for idx, val in enumerate(arr2)}. Sort key: (order.get(x, len(arr2)), x). Time: O(n log n). Use for: custom ordering, priority-based sorting with fallback.

Sorting Pattern: Sort transformed array. Apply quadratic function f(x)=ax²+bx+c to sorted array. Result is sorted in one pass using two-pointer from both ends. If a>=0, larger values at ends, fill result from back. If a<0, larger values in middle, fill from front. Time: O(n) after O(n log n) sort. Use two-pointer merge for O(n) if already sorted.

Sorting Pattern: Lower bound complexity O(n log n) for comparison sorts. Proof: decision tree has n! leaves (permutations), height >= log2(n!) = O(n log n) by Stirling's. Any comparison-based sort needs Omega(n log n) comparisons. Non-comparison sorts (counting, radix, bucket) bypass this by exploiting structure of keys. Know when to use which: comparison sorts are general, non-comparison sorts need assumptions.
